Facts.text: Theodore I's reign as Catholic Pope ends with his death
Robert II of France names his son Henry I as junior King of the Franks
Dutch Council of State replaced by Council of Beroerten
English colonists establish the 1st permanent English settlement in America at Jamestown. Unknown to them they have landed amidst the worst drought in 800 years.
The Protestant Union is founded in Auhausen
Admiral Adam Westerwolt conquers Batticaloa, Ceylon
Turkish Grand Vizier Köprülü Fazıl Ahmed attacks 120,000 Donau soldiers
England & Netherlands declare war on France & Spain
First Battle of Cape Finisterre: British fleet under Admiral George Anson defeats a French 30-ship convoy commanded by Admiral de la Jonquière off Cape Finisterre in northwest Spain
British government disbands American import duty on tea
Paraguay gains independence from Spain (National Day)
Illustrated London News; the world's first illustrated weekly newspaper, begins publication
Utrecht-Arnhem Railway opens
Mindon is crowned as King of Burma at Mandalay Palace, Burma
The Canellas meteorite, an 859-gram chondrite-type meteorite, strikes the earth near Barcelona, Spain
Adolphe Nicole of Switzerland patents the chronograph
American Civil War: The Battle of Jackson, Mississippi takes place
Japanese Boshin War: end of the Battle of Utsunomiya Castle, former Shogunate forces withdraw northward to Aizu by way of Nikkō
First admission charge at a football game, Harvard beats University of McGill (Montreal) 3-0
Vaseline is granted a patent (U.S. Patent 127,568)
The first group of 463 Indian indentured labourers arrive in Fiji aboard the Leonidas
Anti-Monopoly Party forms in the US
The National Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Children (NSPCC) is launched in London, England, led by Benjamin Waugh
Fire in Boston bleachers spreads to 170 adjoining buildings
Lowest US temperature in May recorded (-10°F /-23°C at Climax, Colorado)
Oldest continuously operating movie theater in the world, the State Theatre first opens in Washington, Iowa (Guinness World Records)
Flagpole at the White Sox ballpark breaks during pennant-raising
Sweden adopts universal suffrage for elections to its lower house and proportional representation for both houses
First passenger flight in an airplane
Canada authorizes issuing of silver dollar coins
Frans Hals museum opens in Haarlem, Netherlands
Chic Jim Scott no-hits Cleve, gives up 2 hits in 10th & loses 1-0
Sunday baseball is made legal in Washington, D.C.
Pope Benedictus XV publishes encyclical In hac tanta
Giants inform the Yankees that the lease allowing them to play in the Polo Grounds will not be renewed at the end of the 1920 season
Washington SenatorWalter Johnsonwins his 300th game vs Detroit
Florence Allen is the first female judge to sentence a man to death in Ohio
"Ain't She Sweet?" by Ben Bernie hits #1 on the singles chart
German ocean liner SS Cap Arcona is launched at the Blohm & Voss shipyard in Hamburg
The University of Chicago's local collegiate organization, Phi Sigma, becomes incorporated under Illinois law as Eta Sigma Phi, the National Honorary Classical Fraternity
John McGraw is knocked down by a taxicab and suffers a broken leg
US Congress designates Carlsbad Caverns in New Mexico a National Park
Ådalen shootings: five people are killed in Ådalen, Sweden, as soldiers open fire on an unarmed trade union demonstration
"We Want Beer!" parade in NY
LA's Griffith Planetarium opens, 3rd in US
Plebiscite in the Philippines ratifies independence agreement
England soccer team beats Nazi-Germany, 6-3
Lina Medina of Peru becomes the world's youngest confirmed mother in medical history at the age of five
Admiral Johannes Furstner, Royal Dutch Navy, departs to England
British Local Defence Volunteers forms, an armed citizen militia designed to support the British Army during the Second World War. It is later renamed the Home Guard.
German breakthrough at Sedan
Lord Beaverbrook appointed British minister of aircraft production
Nazis bomb Rotterdam (600-900 dead), Netherlands surrenders to Germany
3,600 Parisian Jews arrested
US Women's Army Auxiliary Corps (WAAC) forms
Australian Hospital Ship Centaur sinks off the coast of Queensland after being struck by torpedo fired by Japanese submarine; 268 of 332 medical personnel and civilian crew aboard die
91 German bombers harass Bristol
British troops occupy Kohima
Kamikaze Zero strikes US aircraft carrier Enterprise
US offensive on Okinawa, Sugar Loaf conquered
Israeli Radio Station Kol Yisrael's 1st broadcast
Jordan's Arab League captures Atarot, north of Jerusalem
US grants Israel de facto recognition
US performs atmospheric nuclear test at Enewetak Atoll
WBEN (now WIVB) TV channel 4 in Buffalo, NY (CBS) begins broadcasting
Pitts Johnny Hopp goes 6 for 6 including 2 HRs
"The Ernie Kovacs Show", a television variety program debuts on NBC-TV
Belgium shortens military conscription from 20 to 18 months
US performs nuclear test at Pacific Ocean
Warsaw Pact is signed by the Soviet Union, Albania, Bulgaria, Czechoslovakia, East Germany, Hungary, Poland and Romania
USSR launch 1st (unmanned) space capsule
Virgil Thomson's "Missa Pro Defunctis" premieres in Potsdam, New York
Bus with 1st group of Freedom Riders bombed & burned in Alabama
Sarvepalli Radhakrishnan elected second President of India
US performs atmospheric nuclear test at Christmas Island
Kuwait is 111th member of the United Nations
17th Cannes Film Festival: "The Umbrellas of Cherbourg" directed by Jacques Demy wins the Grand Prix du Festival International du Film
2nd Chinese atom bomb explodes
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
"A Lover's Concerto" novelty single by Mrs Miller hits #95
1st reported monitoring of pirate radio station WBBH (NJ)
Beatles announce formation of Apple Corp
Abortion & contraception legalized in Canada
Last Chevrolet Corvair built
Militant leftist Red Army Faction leaderAndreas Baaderfreed from prison after serving 2 years in West Berlin
NYC local newspaper "Our Town" begins publishing
24th Emmy Awards: "All in the Family", Carrol O'Conner & Jean Stapleton win
A 13-year old Catholic girl is shot dead by Loyalist paramilitaries in Ballymurphy, Belfast
French film "Day for Night" written and directed by François Truffaut, starring Jacqueline Bisset and Jean-Pierre Léaud premieres at Cannes (Academy Award Best Foreign Language Film)
Gold hits record $102.50 an ounce in London
Skylab launched, 1st Space Station
US Supreme court approves equal rights to females in military
Underground America Day is 1st observed to honor the 6,000 Americans that make their homes in the Earth
Cult feminist film "Jeanne Dielman, 23 Quai du Commerce, 1080 Bruxelles" written and directed by Chantal Ackerman and starring Delphine Seyrig premieres at Cannes
French press reports massive deportation from Cambodia
US forces raid Cambodian island of Koh Tang to free Mayaguez ship
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Lowell Thomas ends 46 years as radio network reporter
Oil tanker Urqui Ola explodes off Spanish coast
English football international Bobby Moore retires
KC Royals Jim Colborn no-hits Texas Rangers, 6-0
Netherlands State Delta Kappa Gamma Society forms
Stanley Cup Final, Boston Garden, Boston, MA: Jacques Lemaire scores twice as Montreal Canadiens edge Boston Bruins, 2-1 in OT for a 4 game sweep and back-to-back titles
First round of the presidential elections in Upper Volta
"Kids Are All Right" rockumentary film featuring The Who premieres at the Cannes Film Festival
Bucky Dent hits an inside park HR, Royals walk 14 Yanks including 5 with bases loaded, Yanks win 16-3
Department of Health & Human Services begins operation
NASA launches space vehicle S-192
Guinea adopts constitution
"She Blinded Me with Science" by Thomas Dolby hits #5
Pride of Baltimore lost at sea.
"Little Shop of Horrors" is released in Germany
Colt revolver (Peacemaker) of 1873 sells for $242,000

Carrollton bus collision: a drunk driver going the wrong way on Interstate 71 near Carrollton, Kentucky, United States hits a converted school bus carrying a church youth group. The crash and ensuing fire kill 27.
Jose Oquendo is the first non-pitcher in 20 years to get a decision in a baseball game as he and the St. Louis Cardinals lose to the Braves 7-5 in 19 innings
1st Tour de Trump bicycle race run (Atlanta)
Demonstration for democratic reforms in Beijing's Tiananmen square
Final TV episode of "Family Ties" airs on NBC with a 36 million audience
Moonlighting, TV Crime Drama last airs on ABC
Dow Jones Industrial Average hits a record 2,821.53
42 die in a train collision in Japan
Robert Gates is nominated for the position of Director of Central Intelligence
World's Largest Burrito created at 1,126 lbs
WIBC Bowling Queens won by Cindy Coburn-Carroll
At 43 years and 162 days, Manchester City goalkeeper John Burridge becomes the oldest player to appear in an EPL match in a 3-2 home defeat to Queens Park Rangers
"Yi Yi" Taiwanese film by Edward Yang debuts at Cannes
The Constitutional Court of South Korea overturns the impeachment of President Roh Moo-hyun

Former USS America (CV-66), a decommissioned supercarrier of the US Navy, deliberately sunk in the Atlantic Ocean after four weeks of live-fire exercises. Largest ship ever to be disposed of as a target in a military exercise.
The 51,700 seat Aviva Stadium is officially opened on the site of the famous old Lansdowne Road Stadium in Dublin by Irish Prime Minister, Brian Cowan
1,500 Palestinian prisoners in Israeli prisons agree to end mass hunger strike
Stanford University scientists develop a prototype bionic eye
Brazil becomes the 15th country to legalize same-sex marriage
Boulder City Council in Colorado votes to ban assault weapons
Chinese mountain climber and double amputee Xia Boyu reaches the summit of Mt Everest
Indian politician Shashi Tharoor is charged by police with driving his wife to commit suicide
Successful memory transfer in snails achieved by scientists from University of California published in journal "eNeuro"
United States moves its Israeli embassy from Tel Aviv to Jerusalem
US Supreme Court strikes down federal law banning sports gambling in most states
Huge protests closes schools in Kashmir after the rape of a three-year-old girl
San Francisco is the first city to vote to ban use of facial recognition
Steve Bullock, US Governor of Montana announces his run for Presidential office
Wikipedia confirms China has banned all versions of its site
Former pandemic agency head Rick Bright testifies to a US House Committee "without better planning, 2020 could be the darkest winter in modern history"
Global death toll from COVID-19 passes 300,000 with 4.4 million confirmed infections
18-year-old gunman kills 10 people and wounds three at a Tops supermarket in east Buffalo, New York, in a racially motivating attack
Surrealist artist Man Ray's photograph "Le Violon d'Ingres" sells for $12.4 million, making it the most expensive ever sold at auction
US records one million COVID-19 deaths, with The White House ordering flags to fly at half mast through the weekend
Cyclone Mocha makes landfall on Rakhine coast, Myanmar, near southern border of Bangladesh with at least 400 killed. Second strongest storm on record in the North Indian Ocean.
Thai General Election: Military-backed government loses out to other parties with the Move Forward Party securing 36% of the vote
Google unveils new generative AI features at its annual conference, forcing users to view AI Overviews at the top of search results despite them being factually incorrect 60% of the time
Battle of Lewes, Second Barons' War: Simon de Montfort the Younger, Earl of Leicester, defeats English KingHenry III
Coronation ofCharles VIIIof France ("Charles the Affable")
Battle of Agnadello, French forces underLouis XIIdefeat the Venetians in Northern Italy
Henry IV of Franceis assassinated by fanatical Catholic François Ravaillac who stabs him to death in the streets of Paris
Louis XIVbecomes King of France aged 4
Great Northern War: Swedish troops under KingCharles XIIoccupy Warsaw, Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth
English country doctorEdward Jenneradministers his revolutionary cowpox-based vaccine for smallpox in Berkeley, Gloucestershire
Friedrich von Schiller's translation ofWilliam Shakespeare's play "Macbeth" premieres in Weimar
Meriwether LewisandWilliam Clark's expedition sets out from St. Louis for the Pacific Coast, commissioned byThomas Jefferson
Felix Mendelssohn's concert overture "Hebrides" premieres in London, England
Land surveyor, newspaper publisher and inventorGail Bordenpatents his process for condensed milk
British sailorDavid Beattyis promoted to sub-lieutenant
Great Britain signs treaty with Ethiopian EmperorMenelik IIsettling border issues with British Somaliland
Italian tenorEnrico Carusomakes his Covent Garden debut oppositeNellie MelbainGiuseppe Verdi's opera "Rigoletto" in London
PresidentTheodore Rooseveltvisits San Francisco
Washington SenatorWalter Johnsonends MLB record scoreless streak at 56 innings
Virginia Woolf's novel "Mrs Dalloway" is published by The Hogarth Press
"The Adventures of Robin Hood", directed by Michael Curtiz and William Keighley, and starringErrol FlynnandOlivia de Havillandpremieres
Boston Red SoxJimmie FoxxHR goes over the left field roof of Comiskey Park in Chicago
World premiere performance ofAaron Copland's "Lincoln Portrait" with: Andre Kostelanetz conducting the Cincinnati Symphony in Cincinnati, Ohio
GeneralRommel, Speidel & von Stulpnagel attempt to assassinateHitler

PhysicianJoseph G. Hamiltoninjects misdiagnosed cancer patientAlbert Stevens(CAL-1) with 131 kBq (3.55 µCi) of plutonium without his knowledge. Stevens lives another 20 years, surviving the highest known accumulated radiation dose in any human
Paul Hindemith's requiem "For Those We Love" premieres
US PresidentHarry Trumansigns bill establishing a rocket test range at Cape Canaveral
Stirling Mosswins the 1961 Monaco Grand Prix
Ex-president of YugoslaviaMilovan Djilassentenced to 5 years in jail
NY YankeeMickey Mantlehits career HR #500 off Baltimore Orioles' Stu Miller
Czech government announces liberalizing reforms underAlexander Dubček
RAF-leaderAndreas Baadersentenced to 3 years in West Berlin
Harry Blackmun(61), nominated byRichard Nixon, earns seat on US Supreme Court after confirmation by US Senate, 94-0; serves until 1994
InWillie Mays' 1st game as New York Met, his homer beats San Francisco Giants, 5-4 (his former team of over 20 years), at Shea Stadium, New York
"Stalker", Russian film directed byAndrei Tarkovsky, starring Alexander Kaidanovsky and Anatoli Solonitsyn, is released
Rosa Motaruns female world record 20k (1:06:55.5)
Netherlands Institute for War Documentation publishesAnne Frank's complete diary
Reggie Jacksonhit his 537th HR passingMickey Mantleinto 6th place
Minnesota TwinKirby Puckettbecomes 1st player to hit 6 doubles across two consecutive games since St. Louis Cardinal Red Schoendienst in 1948
46th time opposing pitchers hit HR,Fernando Valenzuela(Dodgers)/Gross (Expos)
Winnie Mandelais sentenced to six years and is freed pending appeal for complicity in the kidnapping and beating of four youths, one of whom dies
Dave Winfieldpasses Frank Robinson for 12th on RBI list with 1,617
Dalai Lamaproclaims 6-year-oldGedhun Choekyi Nyima11th reincarnation of Panchen Lama, Tibet's 2nd most senior spiritual leader
Eddie Murrayof Indians hits his 463rd career home run (ties for 18th)
New York Yankees' controversial pitcherDwight Goodenno-hits Seattle Mariners, 2-0 at Yankee Stadium
Baseball's Executive Council suspends NY Yankees ownerGeorge Steinbrennerfor his suing other team owners
Seinfeld's final two-part episode "The Finale" airs on NBC-TV to 76.3 million viewers, with commercials priced at $2 million for 30 seconds
Laureus World Sports Awards, Grimaldi Forum, Monaco: Sportsman:Michael Schumacher; Sportswoman:Jennifer Capriati; Team: Australian Men's National Cricket team
PopeBenedict XVIobserves his first beatification, elevating Blessed Marianne of Molokai on the road to canonization into sainthood
René Prévalsworn in as President of Haiti for the second time
Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie’s novel "Americanah" is published
Dan Brown's fourth novel starring Robert Langdon (The Da Vinci Code), titled "Inferno", is released and instantly becomes a bestseller
Gabriel Medinabecomes the first surfer ever to land the move "Backflip" in competition
Melania Trump, wife of US President Donald Trump, is treated in hospital for a benign kidney condition

Octavia E. Butlerreceives the first-ever Infinity Award from the Science Fiction and Fantasy Writers Association, an award given to those who have died before they could be considered for a Grand Master award
Delegates gather in Philadelphia to draw up the Constitution of the United States
David Ben-Guriondeclares Israel independent from British administration, Golda Meir one of the signatories
Charles IV, King of Bohemia (1346-78) and Holy Roman Emperor (1355-78), born in Prague (d. 1378)
Margaret of Valois, Queen of France, wife of Henry IV (1572-99), daughter of Henry II, born in Château de Saint-Germain-en-Laye, France (d. 1615)
Francesco Rasi, Italian composer, singer, poet, and theorbo player, born in Arezzo, Grand Duchy of Tuscany (d. 1621)
Johann Philipp Förtsch, German Baroque composer, statesman, and physician, born in Wertheim am Main, Wertheim County, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1732)
Peder Horrebow, Danish astronomer who invented the Horrebow-Talcott Method, a method to determine a location's latitude from the stars, born in Løgstør, Jutland, Denmark (d. 1764)
Hans Joachim von Zieten, Prussian cavalry general, born in Wustrau, Brandenburg (d. 1786)
William Emerson, British mathematician known for his work on fluxions, born in Hurworth, England (d. 1782)
Antonio Teixeira, Portuguese composer, born in Lisbon, Portugal (d. after 1769)
Adolf Frederik, King of Sweden (1751-70), born in Gottorp, Schleswig, Duchy of Schleswig (d. 1771)
Ludovico Manin, Venetian politician, last Doge of Venice (1789-97), born in Venice, Republic of Venice (d. 1802)
Thomas Gainsborough, English leading portrait painter (Blue Boy), born in Sudbury, England (d. 1788) baptism date[1]
George Macartney, 1st Earl Macartney, British statesman and the first British ambassador to China (1792-96), born in Loughguile, Northern Ireland (d. 1806)
Albrecht Thaer, German agronomist and founder of modern scientific agriculture (Principles of Rational Agriculture), born in Celle, Electorate of Hanover  (d. 1828)[1][2]
Timothy Dwight, American theologian, poet and member of the Hartford Wits literary group, born in Northampton, Massachusetts (d. 1817)
Robert Owen, Welsh factory owner and utopian socialist, born in Newtown, Wales (d. 1858)
Friedrich von Raumer, German historian, travel writer (Italy and the Italians) and politician, born in Wörlitz (d. 1873)
František Palacký "Father of the Nation", Czech historian and politician (led Czech National Revival), born in Hodslavice, Habsburg Empire (d. 1876)
Johann Peter Emilius Hartmann, Danish Romantic composer, born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 1900)
Emilie Mayer, German composer of Romantic music, born in Friedland, Mecklenburg-Vorpommern, Swedish Pomerania (d. 1883)
Charles Beyer, German-British locomotive designer and chief engineer at Beyer, Peacock & Co, born in Plauen, Saxony (d. 1876)
Gualtiero Sanelli, Italian composer, born in Parma, Duchy of Parma and Piacenza (d. 1861)
Alexander Kaufmann, German poet and folklorist, born in Bonn, Rhine Province, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1893)
George Pierce Doles, American businessman and Brigadier General in the Confederate Army, born in Milledgeville, Georgia (d. 1864)
Rudolf Lipschitz, German mathematician known for his work on the Lipschitz condition, born in Königsberg, East Pussia (d. 1903)
James Patrick Major, American Brigadier General in the Confederate Army who was part of the Red River campaign, born in Fayette, Missouri (d. 1877)
Sir Squire Bancroft [Butterfield], British actor and manager known for creating 'drawing-room comedy' (Haymarket), born in Rotherhithe, London (d. 1926)
Arnold Kerdijk, Dutch liberal politician (founder Social Weekly newspaper), born in Rotterdam. Netherlands (d. 1905)
Pieter Cort van de Linden, Dutch politician (Prime Minister of Netherlands 1913-18), born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1935)
John Ware, American-Canadian black rancher and cowboy born into slavery, born in North Texas (d. 1905)[1]
John Charles Fields, Canadian mathematician and founder of the Fields Medal, born in Hamilton, Ontario (d. 1932)
Eleanor Everest Freer, American opera composer (The Legend of the Piper), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1942)
Kurt Eisner, German socialist politician, revolutionary and journalist (President of the People's State of Bavaria 1918-19), born in Berlin, Prussia (d. 1919)
Elia Dalla Costa, Italian cardinal who helped organize a rescue network for Jewish people during the Holocaust, born in Villaverla, Italy (d. 1961)
J. L. Wilkinson, American Baseball HOF executive (founder All Nations Baseball club 1912 & NgL Kansas City Monarchs 1920), born in Algona, Iowa (d. 1964)
Wilhelm List, German field marshal during World War Ⅱ who was convicted of war crimes, born in Oberkirchberg, Germany (d. 1971)
George Murray Hulbert, American lawyer, jurist, and politician (NY Representative), born in Rochester, New York (d. 1950)
Jan Olieslagers, Belgian aviation and motorcycle pioneer (WWI flying ace, Antwerp Devil), born in Antwerp, Belgium (d. 1942)
Julian Eltinge [Dalton], American vaudeville, Broadway, and silent film female impersonator star (The Crinoline Girl), born in Newtownville, Massachusetts (d. 1941)
Otto Klemperer, German-American conductor (Los Angeles Philharmonic, 1933-39), and composer (Das Ziel), born in Breslau, Kingdom of Prussia, German Empire (d. 1973)
Miles Mander, British actor (Tower of London, Wuthering Heights, Murder!), born in Wolverhampton, Staffordshire, England (d. 1946)
Egon Kornauth, Austrian composer, born in Olmütz, Kingdom of Moravia, Austria-Hungarian (d. 1959)
Arthur Vincent Lourie, Russian composer, born in Propoysk, Russia (d. 1966)
Felix Petyrek, Austrian composer, born in Brno, Kingdom of Moravia, Austria-Hungary (d. 1951)
Ivan Wyschnegradsky, Russian microtonal composer, born in Saint Petersburg, Russia (d. 1979)
Louis Verneuil, French playwright, screenwriter and actor (My Life with Caroline, Murder for Sale), born in Paris, France (d. 1952)[1][2]
Lew Lehr, American comedian (Stop Me if I heard this One), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1950)
Renato Lunelli, composer Italian organist, composer, and musicologist, born in Trent, Austria-Hungary (d. 1967)
Ed Ricketts, American marine biologist (Between Pacific Tides, Sea of Cortez), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1948)
Sidney Bechet, American jazz clarinetist, saxophonist, bandleader, and composer ("Petite Fleur"), born in New Orleans, Louisiana (d. 1959)
Bonifacio Gil Garcia, Spanish composer and folklorist, born in Santo Domingo de la Calzada, Spain (d. 1964)
[Arthur] Zutty Singleton, American jazz drummer (Louis Armstrong's Hot Five), born in Bunkie, Louisiana (d. 1975)
Earle Combs, American Baseball HOF center fielder (9 × World Series 1927, 28, 32, 36–39, 41, 43; New York Yankees), born in Richmond, Kentucky (d. 1976)
Pierre Victor Auger, French physicist who is known for the discovery of giant air showers, born in Paris, France (d. 1993)[1]
Stelios Perpiniadis, Greek folk singer, songwriter and guitarist, born in Tinos, Greece (d. 1977)
Edgar Wind, German art historian specializing in the Renaissance and the first professor of art history at Oxford University, born in Berlin, Germany (d. 1971)
Hal Borland [Harold Glen Borland], American author and nature journalist (The Dog Who Came to Stay, When the Legends Die), born in Sterling, Nebraska (d. 1978)[1][2]
Leo Smit, Dutch composer who died in a Nazi extermination camp, born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1943)
Walter Rehberg, Swiss concert pianist, composer, and musicologist, born in Geneva, Switzerland (d. 1957)
Robert Ritter, German child psychologist who classified the Roma and Sinti populations for persecution under the Third Reich, born in Aachen, German Empire (d. 1951)[1]
Billie Dove [Lilian Bohny], American actress (Black Pirate, Stolen Bride), born in New York City (d. 1997)
Hans Albert Einstein, Swiss-American professor and engineer, born in Bern, Switzerland (d. 1973)
Marcel Junod, Swiss physician and International Committee of the Red Cross delegate (1935-59), born in Neuchâtel, Switzerland (d. 1961)
Jean Daniélou, French Jesuit, cardinal and theologian (Prayer as a Political Problem), born in Neuilly-sur-Seine, France (d. 1974)[1][2]
Hastings Kamuzu Banda, Prime Minister then 1st President of Malawi (1964-94), born near Kasungu, British Central Africa (d. 1997)
James Flavin, American actor (King Kong, In Cold Blood, Man With a Camera), born in Portland, Maine (d. 1976)
Dick Bentley, Australian comedian and actor (The Glums), born in Melbourne, Australia (d. 1995)
Hans von der Groeben, German diplomat and member of the European Commission (1958-67), born in Langheim, East Prussia (d. 2005)
Johnny Moss, American poker player known as the "Grand Old Man of Poker", born in Marshall, Texas (d. 1995)
Mohammed Ayub Khan, Pakistani general and politician (1958 coup, President of Pakistan 1958-69), born in Rehana, British India (d. 1974)
Ken Viljoen, South African cricket batsman (27 Tests, 2 x 100, HS 124; Griqualand West, Orange Free State, Transvaal), born in Windsorton, South Africa (d. 1974)
Hans Vogt, German composer, born in Danzig, Kingdom of Prussia, German Empire (d. 1992)
Marguerite Fawdry, English museum curator (started Pollocks Toy Museum), born in Bexleyheath, England (d. 1995)
Del Moore, American comedian and actor (Life With Elizabeth; Bachelor Father - "Cal"; Cinderfella), born in Pensacola, Florida (d. 1970)
Lance Dossor, British-Australian concert pianist, born in Weston-super-Mare, England (d. 2005)
Marco Zanuso, Italian Modernist architect and product designer, born in Milan, Italy (d. 2001)
Lou Harrison, American composer (La Koro Sutro), born in Portland, Oregon (d. 2003)[1]
Norman Luboff, American vocal arranger, music publisher, and choir director (Norman Luboff Choir - "Yellow Bird"), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1987)
Arthur McIntyre, English cricket wicket-keeper (3 Tests, 8 dismissals; Surrey CCC, MCC), born in London, England (d. 2009)
Heloise Bowles, (Cruse) American advice columnist (Hints from Heloise), born in Fort Worth, Texas (d. 1977)
John Hope, American meteorologist and hurricane expert, born in Stowell, Pennsylvania (d. 2002)
Solange Chaput-Rolland, French politician, senator, journalist and author (Disunited Country), born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 2001)[1]
Arve Opsahl, Norwegian actor and stand-up comedian (Mot i brøstet, Olsen-banden), born in Oslo, Norway (d. 2007)
Agha Hasan Abedi, Pakistani banker and felon (BCCI), born in Lucknow, British India (d. 1995)
Richard Deacon, American actor (The Dick Van Dyke Show - "Mel Cooley"), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1984)
Adnan Pachachi, Iraqi and Emirati politician and diplomat (Iraq Minister of Foreign Affairs during Six-Day War), born in Baghdad, Iraq (d. 2019)
Brad Anderson, American cartoonist (Marmaduke), born in Jamestown, New York (d. 2015)
Joly Braga Santos, Portuguese composer and conductor, born in Lisbon, Portugal (d. 1988)
Kaiti Grey [Athanasia Gizili], Greek pop singer, born in Mytilinioi, Samos, Greece (d. 2025)
Sonia d’Artois, British Canadian WWII resistance agent, born in Eastchurch, Kent (d. 2014)
Al Porcino, American big band jazz and session trumpeter (Woody Herman, Stan Kenton), born in New York City (d. 2013)
Boris Parsadanian, Soviet Armenian-Estonian violinist, and composer, born in Kislovodsk, Russian SFSR (d. 1997)
Oona O'Neill, Lady Chaplin, Bermudian-born British actress, daughter of Eugene O'Neill, and wife of Charlie Chaplin, born in Warwick Parish, Bermuda (d. 1991)
Patrice Munsel [Munsil], American operatic soprano (Metropolitan Opera, 1943-58; Patrice Munsel Show), born in Spokane, Washington (d. 2016)
Tristram Ogilvie Cary, British-Australian composer, electronic music pioneer, and educator, born in Oxford, England (d. 2008)
Čestmír Gregor, Czech composer, born in Brno, Czechoslovakia (d. 2011)
Eric Morecambe [John Bartholomew], British comedian (Morecambe & Wise, Picadilly Palace), born in Morecambe, Lancashire (d. 1984)
Herbert W. Franke, Austrian scientist, writer, and computer art pioneer, born in Vienna, Austria (d. 2022)
Shirley Spork, American golfer and administrator (co-founder LPGA; Women's PGA C'ship runner-up 1962), born in Detroit, Michigan (d. 2022)
Frederik H. Kreuger, Dutch scientist, inventor, and writer, born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 2015)
Will "Dub" Jones, American bass singer (The Coasters - "Charlie Brown"), born in Shreveport, Louisiana (d. 2000)
Barbara Branden, Canadian writer and lecturer (The Passion of Ayn Rand, Who is Ayn Rand?), born in Winnipeg (d. 2013)[1][2]
Gump Worsley, Canadian ice hockey player, born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 2007)
Henry McGee, British actor and comedian (No, That's Me Over Here!, The Benny Hill Show), born in South Kensington, London (d. 2006)
Edward Regan, American Republican politician and Comptroller of New York (1979-93), born in Plainfield, New Jersey (d. 2014)
María Irene Fornés, Cuban-American playwright (Fefu and Her Friends, Enter the Night), born in Havana, Cuba (d. 2018)[1]
Władysław Słowiński, Polish contemporary classical composer, born in Sadlno, Poland
Alvin Lucier, American composer of experimental music (I Am Sitting In A Room; Music On A Long Thin Wire), educator, and music director, born in Nashua, New Hampshire (d. 2021)
Loek Elfferich, Dutch journalist and historian (Treason in Rotterdam), born in Rotterdam, Netherlands (d. 1992)
Richard Estes, American artist and photorealist painter (Telephone Booths), born in Kewanee, Illinois
Robert Bechtle, American photorealist painter, born in San Francisco, California (d. 2020)
